title: Auto Capture on Fulfillment
summary: Payment auto-capture on fulfillment does NOT work natively for B2B, even when location terms are set to 'due on fulfillment'. A specific API sequence can enable it, but partial fulfillments still require manual steps.
key_points: Not automatic for B2B natively — differs from DTC behavior even with 'due on fulfillment' terms
The reason: payment isn't authorized at order placement, so the buyer's revolving credit isn't tied up

Auto-capture CAN be triggered automatically if ALL of these are true: (1) order created with 'due on fulfillment' terms, (2) funds authorized via orderCreateMandatePayment, (3) order is COMPLETELY fulfilled
Partial fulfillments still require manual intervention in all cases

title: Terms & Card-on-File Requirements
summary: When a location has payment terms of any kind, a card MUST be saved to the location profile before an order can be placed. This ensures Shopify can charge the card later, outside the initial authorization window.
key_points: If the location has ANY terms (Net 30, Due on Fulfillment, etc.), a saved card is required to place an order
This applies to both storefront and admin-created orders
The requirement ensures Shopify can charge the card after the authorization window closes
Without a saved card, the order submission will fail at checkout

title: No Terms Location — Payment Behavior
summary: Locations with 'No Terms' get a 30-day card authorization, but auto-capture (full or partial) is still not possible. Custom amounts can be captured manually, and customers cannot 'Pay Now' on invoices.
key_points: Card IS authorized for ~30 days when terms are 'None'
Auto-capture (full or partial) is still not possible natively
Custom amounts can be captured up to the order total
Authorization expiry: funds can still be captured after the 30-day window — Shopify re-authorizes behind the scenes
Customers CANNOT 'Pay Now' on invoices for No Terms locations
Different cards can be used at order time without being saved to the location (since an authorization exists)

title: Deleting Saved Cards — Safe for Merchants
summary: Customers can freely delete saved cards from their location profile without risk to the merchant. The card associated with an existing order can still be charged even after deletion.
key_points: Customers can delete saved cards at any time — safe for merchants
A deleted card associated with an existing order can still be charged up to the order total
A different card on file for the same location can also be used to capture payment
Cards entered directly in Admin by a staff member can also be used to capture

title: Credit Card Authorization Windows
summary: When cards are authorized, Shopify automatically gives the longest possible authorization window (e.g., 30 days for Visa). Capturing within different windows incurs different rates — no configuration options exist. Authorizations are not always provided for B2B orders.
key_points: Authorization window is automatic — no configuration options
Capturing within standard window (e.g., 7 days for Visa) = standard fees; capturing in extended window (8–30 days) = higher rate, applied automatically
DTC On Fulfillment (No Terms): Authorization provided — yes, up to 30 days
B2B On Fulfillment (No Terms): Authorization provided — yes, up to 30 days
B2B On Fulfillment (Due on Fulfillment terms): Authorization NOT provided — order shows 'unauthorized'
B2B On Fulfillment (Due in X Days terms): Authorization NOT provided — order shows 'payment pending'

title: Variable Platform Fee (VPF) — How It's Determined
summary: VPF eligibility depends primarily on HOW payment is taken, not how the order is created. Orders where payment is brokered by Shopify (CC, ACH, Admin, POS) qualify; imported orders with external payment typically do not.
key_points: Primary factor: Is payment brokered by Shopify? (CC, ACH, Admin, POS) → VPF applies at the rate for the payment channel
B2B on POS with CC payment → charged POS VPF (not B2B VPF)
Offline payment / marked as paid / custom payment method → look at how the ORDER was brokered (storefront, admin draft, POS) → B2B VPF applies
Imported orders (created in ERP/custom app, imported to Shopify after the fact) → typically NOT VPF eligible, even if marked as paid in Admin
Exception: if payment for an imported order IS facilitated by Shopify, it becomes VPF eligible
Consult the business's VPF resources for edge cases like POS + B2B app combinations

title: Tap to Pay — B2B Limitations
summary: Tap to Pay works on iPhone and Android only — not iPad. Shopify Payments must not be in Test Mode. B2B Extend works with Tap to Pay on POS, but requires placing the order first and then collecting payment separately.
key_points: iPhone and Android only — iPad is NOT supported
Applies to both the POS app and the Shopify Admin app
Shopify Payments must NOT be in Test Mode
In the Shopify Admin app: only Draft Orders and Quick Orders are supported (not 'normal' orders)
B2B Extend works with Tap to Pay on POS
Workflow: place the order first → find it in Online Orders list → Collect Payment → select Tap to Pay

title: Charging a B2B Saved Card (or ACH) by API
summary: It IS possible to charge a B2B saved payment method (card on file or ACH bank account) via API using orderCreateMandatePayment. Any amount can be authorized — even above the order total — giving flexibility for order edits. ACH does not support auth-only.
key_points: Use orderCreateMandatePayment mutation — NOT orderCapture directly (funds aren't pre-authorized for terms orders)
Requires: Order ID, Mandate ID (from order query), and a unique Idempotency Key
autoCapture: false = authorize only; autoCapture: true = authorize and capture immediately
You can authorize an amount LARGER than the order total — useful if you anticipate order edits (e.g., shipping changes); excess triggers a refund flag
After auth-only, capture using orderCapture mutation; or void auth and re-auth using orderCreateMandatePayment
ACH bank account: cannot do auth-only — even with autoCapture: false, funds will be captured
Multiple saved payment methods on a location = multiple mandates; ACH mandate is identifiable by lack of card detail in the order query response

code_samples.label: Authorize or Capture (orderCreateMandatePayment)
Capture After Auth (orderCapture)

doc_section: Charging a B2B Saved Payment Method (Card on File) by API

title: Creating a B2B Order by API
summary: B2B orders can be created via two approaches: a two-step draft order flow (preferred when using purchasing entity fields to link Company/Location/Contact), or a single-step orderCreate mutation (preferred for ERP imports). Each has distinct payment options.
key_points: Two-step flow: draftOrderCreate → draftOrderComplete — use when you need purchasingEntity fields (Company, Location, Contact); the default card on file can be charged after completion
Single-step: orderCreate mutation — preferred for importing orders from ERP; requires an authorization token from a custom app
After single-step order creation, the order is NOT immediately payable in the customer portal — must pay via API (orderCreateMandatePayment), Admin (credit card or manual), or Send Invoice
Payment terms can be set via paymentTermsTemplateId in the draft order input

code_samples.label: Step 1 — Create Draft Order (with B2B purchasingEntity)
Step 2 — Complete Draft Order
Single-Step — orderCreate (ERP imports)

doc_section: Creating a B2B Order by API

title: Creating B2B Catalogs via API
summary: Catalogs are created in four sequential steps via GraphQL API: create Price List, create Catalog (optionally linking a Company Location), create Publication, then update the Publication to assign products.
key_points: Step 1: PriceListCreate — defines pricing rules for the catalog
Step 2: CatalogCreate — creates the catalog; can associate a Company Location directly here; capture the returned Catalog ID
Step 3: PublicationCreate — creates the publication channel
Step 4: PublicationUpdate — assigns products/collections to the publication
Full documentation with code examples available in the B2B Solutions Guide
The existing catalog creation guide is for Markets, not Company Locations specifically — logic is similar but check B2B-specific docs

title: B2B Store Credit — Admin & API
summary: B2B Store Credit (available Aug/Sep 2025+) extends DTC store credit to Company Locations. Credit lives at the location level, shared by all customers at that location. Manageable via Admin UI or API (Admin API 2025-10+).
key_points: Store credit lives at the Company Location level — all buyers at that location share the balance
Enable in Customer Account settings (single toggle for both DTC and B2B — cannot enable for one only)
In Admin: Company Location screen → 'Store credit' section → add/remove credit, set expiration date
Via API: Use Admin API 2025-10, StoreCreditAccount object; key mutations are storeCreditAccountCredit (add) and storeCreditAccountDebit (remove)
IMPORTANT: Available store credit does NOT show up in checkout when the location has payment terms; it DOES appear when paying for a past order

code_samples.label: Credit a Company Location (storeCreditAccountCredit)

title: Draft Orders — Sales Rep Has Full Product Access
summary: When a sales rep creates a manual draft order in Admin, they have access to ALL products in the store — not just what the customer's catalog allows. This is by design to give reps more flexibility.
key_points: Admin draft orders bypass catalog restrictions — reps see all products
The intended behavior: reps should have more choice than what customers can self-serve
Framing for merchants: 'We want sales reps to have full flexibility when creating manual orders'
If this is a concern, consider using the Draftable app to add structure or the Sales Rep Frontend workflow below

title: Draftable — Draft Order Helper App
summary: Draftable extends Admin draft order functionality: customers can apply discount codes, reps can add line item properties (e.g., product customizations), deduct inventory with one click, and reorder line items.
key_points: Create a checkout link from a draft order — lets the customer apply discount codes themselves
Add line item properties or order notes to draft orders (e.g., product customization details)
Multiple of the same SKU with different line item properties — useful for configurable products (e.g., custom shirts with different sizes/colors as separate line items)
Deduct inventory for all draft order line items with one click
Reorder line items within the draft order
Create a draft order from an abandoned checkout, preserving product properties

title: Sales Rep Frontend Order Submission
summary: For merchants with complex product catalogs, give sales reps a storefront-like ordering experience by creating a dedicated Company + Location for reps, with orders automatically routed to draft status for admin review and reassignment.
key_points: Setup: Create a dedicated Company for sales reps; create Location(s) for regional catalogs if needed; set location orders to go to draft; add sales reps as customers at the location
Workflow: Rep logs into storefront → browses and creates order → order submitted as draft → admin staff unlocks pricing and changes the customer to the actual buyer
This gives reps an end-user-like search experience for large catalogs without building a custom admin UI
Optional: use regional Locations to give reps access to region-specific catalogs
doc_section: Sales Rep Front End Order Submission

title: Selling Products Restricted by Shopify Payments
summary: Certain product categories (tobacco, firearms, etc.) cannot be sold via Shopify Payments. A two-store approach — one for unrestricted products (Shopify Payments) and one for restricted (third-party processor) — is the primary workaround.
key_points: Products restricted by Shopify Payments (tobacco, guns, etc.) cannot use Shopify Payments
Two-store approach: Store A = unrestricted products + Shopify Payments; Store B = restricted (and optionally unrestricted) products + third-party processor
The two stores can be partially integrated — see the B2B Field Learnings doc for integration patterns
Consider the impact on B2B catalog management, order history, and customer accounts when using two stores
doc_section: Selling Products Restricted by Shopify Payments

title: Displaying Discounted Prices on Product / Collection Pages
summary: Native Shopify discounts don't display on PDPs/collection pages because discounts are cart-aware. Three approaches exist: Compare At prices in the catalog, metafield-based calculation, or an app like Discountly.
key_points: Native discounts only surface at Cart/Checkout — not on PDPs or collection pages
Option 1 (simple): Upload Compare At prices directly in the catalog — works for straightforward discounts
Option 2 (moderate): Store discount % in metafields, calculate discounted price in Liquid/JS and render on the page — caveat: conflicts if an app is also showing a price
Option 3 (complex): Store discount ID in metafields, use app proxy to query discount amount, update prices via JS after page load
Option 4 (app): Use Discountly or similar — manages discounts outside native Shopify discounting and automatically shows discounted prices on PDPs
doc_section: Displaying Discounted prices on Product/Collection Pages

title: Custom Payment Terms (e.g., '14 Following Month')
summary: Shopify's native payment terms (Net X days, Due on Fulfillment) don't support European-style terms like '14 Following Month'. A custom app pattern using company metafields + paymentTermsSet mutation enables fully custom term logic.
key_points: Native options: Net X days, Due on Fulfillment — 'X Following Month' is NOT natively supported
Solution: store custom term configuration in a company metafield as JSON
A custom app reads the metafield at checkout and applies the computed due date via paymentTermsSet mutation
Different companies can have distinct billing cycle configurations
This requires custom development and a private/custom app

title: Shopify Tax Settings for B2B (US)
summary: US B2B tax collection is determined by three inputs: store-level nexus settings, company location tax settings, and delivery address. The combination of these three — not any single setting — controls whether tax is collected.
key_points: Three inputs determine tax: store-level nexus, company location tax setting, and delivery address
Merchant has nexus + Location set to 'Collect Tax' → tax IS collected
Merchant has NO nexus → tax is NOT collected, regardless of location setting
Location set to 'Don't Collect Tax' → tax is never collected, regardless of nexus
Location set to 'Collect Tax Unless Exemptions Apply' + state exemption on file → tax not collected; no exemption → tax collected (only if merchant has nexus)
If tax is not collected but merchant lacks nexus AND buyer lacks reseller certificate → buyer may owe Use Tax in delivery state
Always recommend merchants consult a tax professional for nexus determination

title: NetSuite ERP Integration Options
summary: Two main connectors for NetSuite + Shopify B2B: the NetSuite-owned connector (simpler, sync-direction limitations) and the Celigo connector (more flexible, bidirectional, extensible). Key limitations: neither syncs B2B catalogs or price lists.
key_points: NetSuite Connector: Product (NS→Shopify), Company (Shopify→NS as customer+sub-customer), Price & Inventory (NS→Shopify), B2B Order (Shopify→NS), Fulfillment (NS→Shopify), Refunds/Adjustments (bidirectional), Payout, Reverse Payment — ~$900/month
NetSuite Connector DOES NOT: sync B2B catalogs/price lists, export B2B order history NS→Shopify, support draft orders (only completed orders sync)
Celigo Connector: same sync types but bidirectional for products and companies; more configurable; data mapping is extensible/customizable
NEITHER connector syncs B2B catalogs or price lists — this must be managed manually or via a custom solution
Celigo is preferred for merchants needing more control over sync behavior

title: SSO / Identity Providers
summary: Shopify supports OpenID Connect for customer account SSO (external identity providers) and SAML for internal Shopify admin user SSO (including Microsoft Entra/Azure AD).
key_points: Customer accounts (B2B buyer login): OpenID Connect (OIDC) — identity provider must meet Shopify's specific requirements
Internal users (Shopify admin staff login): SAML — including Microsoft Entra (formerly Azure AD)
Review Shopify's requirements carefully before committing to a specific IdP — not all OIDC providers work out of the box

title: Site Performance & Real-Time Monitoring
summary: Shopify does not provide server log access or server-level monitoring. Shopify offers basic Core Web Vitals data natively; real-time alerting requires third-party tools like Pingdom, Datadog, or New Relic.
key_points: Shopify does NOT provide access to server logs or support server-level monitoring (e.g., CloudWatch)
Native: Shopify provides Google Core Web Vitals data (LCP, INP, etc.) — no real-time alerting
Third-party options: Pingdom (uptime/alerting), Datadog (APM), New Relic (APM)
Datadog and New Relic both integrate with Shopify — consult their documentation for setup guides

title: Inventory Checks — When They Fire
summary: For products set to NOT continue selling when out of stock, Shopify performs inventory checks at five distinct points in the buyer journey — not just at checkout submission.
key_points: Inventory checks fire at: PDP page load/refresh, Add to Cart click, Cart line item quantity update, Checkout page load/refresh, Submit Order click
Out-of-stock message is displayed to the customer at whichever step the OOS condition is detected
This prevents overselling for products configured with 'Stop selling when out of stock'

title: Themes Built for B2B
summary: The Shopify-native B2B theme is Trade. Several third-party themes also support B2B use cases, though partner validation varies.
key_points: Shopify native B2B theme: Trade
Third-party themes with B2B support: Hyper, Xtra, Yuva, Taiga
Concept theme: not built for B2B natively, but field reports indicate some merchants use it successfully
Always validate B2B-specific features (quantity rules, price display, customer accounts) with the theme developer before recommending

title: Paid Plus Trials — Cautions for Account Teams
summary: Eligible merchants can trial Shopify Plus for $399/month (1–2 months depending on region). Key risk: merchants can self-convert to full Plus without the account team, potentially causing lost deal credit.
key_points: Existing merchants globally: 2-month trial at $399 USD/month
New merchants (US/CA only): 1-month trial at $399 USD/month
Not all Plus features are available during a paid trial — check limitations before setting expectations
RISK: Merchants can self-convert to a full Plus store without account team involvement — AE must close-win the opportunity at least 3 days before trial end to preserve credit
If the store auto-converts before the CW, credit typically will not be given to the account team
